name: wheel
documentation:
# wheel

This library is the reference implementation of the Python wheel packaging
standard, as defined in [PEP 427].

It has two different roles:

1. A [setuptools] extension for building wheels that provides the
   `bdist_wheel` setuptools command
2. A command line tool for working with wheel files

It should be noted that wheel is **not** intended to be used as a library, and
as such there is no stable, public API.

## Documentation

The [documentation] can be found on Read The Docs.

## Code of Conduct

Everyone interacting in the wheel project's codebases, issue trackers, chat
rooms, and mailing lists is expected to follow the [PSF Code of Conduct].

[documentation]: https://wheel.readthedocs.io/
[pep 427]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/
[psf code of conduct]: https://github.com/pypa/.github/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
[setuptools]: https://pypi.org/project/setuptools/

functions.name: wheel.cli.unpack.unpack
documentation:
Unpack a wheel.

Wheel content will be unpacked to {dest}/{name}-{ver}, where {name}
is the package name and {ver} its version.

:param path: The path to the wheel.
:param dest: Destination directory (default to current directory).

documentation: urlsafe_b64decode without padding

modules.name: wheel.pkginfo
documentation:
Tools for reading and writing PKG-INFO / METADATA without caring
about the encoding.

functions.name: wheel.cli.pack.pack
documentation:
Repack a previously unpacked wheel directory into a new wheel file.

The .dist-info/WHEEL file must contain one or more tags so that the target
wheel file name can be determined.

:param directory: The unpacked wheel directory
:param dest_dir: Destination directory (defaults to the current directory)

modules.name: wheel.cli
documentation: Wheel command-line utility.

modules.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._typing
documentation:
For neatly implementing static typing in packaging.

`mypy` - the static type analysis tool we use - uses the `typing` module, which
provides core functionality fundamental to mypy's functioning.

Generally, `typing` would be imported at runtime and used in that fashion -
it acts as a no-op at runtime and does not have any run-time overhead by
design.

As it turns out, `typing` is not vendorable - it uses separate sources for
Python 2/Python 3. Thus, this codebase can not expect it to be present.
To work around this, mypy allows the typing import to be behind a False-y
optional to prevent it from running at runtime and type-comments can be used
to remove the need for the types to be accessible directly during runtime.

This module provides the False-y guard in a nicely named fashion so that a
curious maintainer can reach here to read this.

In packaging, all static-typing related imports should be guarded as follows:

    from packaging._typing import TYPE_CHECKING

    if TYPE_CHECKING:
        from typing import ...

Ref: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/3216

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging._typing.cast
documentation: executed at runtime

documentation: Return the version specifier for a requirement in PEP 345/566 fashion.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.convert_requirements
documentation: Yield Requires-Dist: strings for parsed requirements strings.

functions.name: wheel.metadata.generate_requirements
documentation:
Convert requirements from a setup()-style dictionary to ('Requires-Dist', 'requirement')
and ('Provides-Extra', 'extra') tuples.

extras_require is a dictionary of {extra: [requirements]} as passed to setup(),
using the empty extra {'': [requirements]} to hold install_requires.

documentation: Dedent and convert pkg_info['Description'] to Unicode.

modules.name: wheel.macosx_libfile
documentation:
This module contains function to analyse dynamic library
headers to extract system information

Currently only for MacOSX

Library file on macosx system starts with Mach-O or Fat field.
This can be distinguish by first 32 bites and it is called magic number.
Proper value of magic number is with suffix _MAGIC. Suffix _CIGAM means
reversed bytes order.
Both fields can occur in two types: 32 and 64 bytes.

FAT field inform that this library contains few version of library
(typically for different types version). It contains
information where Mach-O headers starts.

Each section started with Mach-O header contains one library
(So if file starts with this field it contains only one version).

After filed Mach-O there are section fields.
Each of them starts with two fields:
cmd - magic number for this command
cmdsize - total size occupied by this section information.

In this case only sections LC_VERSION_MIN_MACOSX (for macosx 10.13 and earlier)
and LC_BUILD_VERSION (for macosx 10.14 and newer) are interesting,
because them contains information about minimal system version.

Important remarks:
- For fat files this implementation looks for maximum number version.
  It not check if it is 32 or 64 and do not compare it with currently built package.
  So it is possible to false report higher version that needed.
- All structures signatures are taken form macosx header files.
- I think that binary format will be more stable than `otool` output.
  and if apple introduce some changes both implementation will need to be updated.
- The system compile will set the deployment target no lower than
  11.0 for arm64 builds. For "Universal 2" builds use the x86_64 deployment
  target when the arm64 target is 11.0.

functions.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.read_mach_header
documentation:
This funcition parse mach-O header and extract
information about minimal system version

:param lib_file: reference to opened library file with pointer

functions.name: wheel.macosx_libfile.calculate_macosx_platform_tag
documentation:
Calculate proper macosx platform tag basing on files which are included to wheel

Example platform tag `macosx-10.14-x86_64`

A wheel is a built archive format.

Use a fallback value for determining SOABI flags if the needed config
var is unset or unavailable.

functions.name: wheel.bdist_wheel.get_abi_tag

Return the ABI tag based on SOABI (if available) or emulate SOABI
(CPython 2, PyPy).

classes.name: wheel.wheelfile.WheelFile
documentation:
A ZipFile derivative class that also reads SHA-256 hashes from
.dist-info/RECORD and checks any read files against those.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.parse_tag
documentation:
Parses the provided tag (e.g. `py3-none-any`) into a frozenset of Tag instances.

Returning a set is required due to the possibility that the tag is a
compressed tag set.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._warn_keyword_parameter
documentation: Backwards-compatibility with Python 2.7 to allow treating 'warn' as keyword-only.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._abi3_applies
documentation:
Determine if the Python version supports abi3.

PEP 384 was first implemented in Python 3.2.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.cpython_tags
documentation:
Yields the tags for a CPython interpreter.

The tags consist of:
- cp<python_version>-<abi>-<platform>
- cp<python_version>-abi3-<platform>
- cp<python_version>-none-<platform>
- cp<less than python_version>-abi3-<platform>  # Older Python versions down to 3.2.

If python_version only specifies a major version then user-provided ABIs and
the 'none' ABItag will be used.

If 'abi3' or 'none' are specified in 'abis' then they will be yielded at
their normal position and not at the beginning.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.generic_tags
documentation:
Yields the tags for a generic interpreter.

The tags consist of:
- <interpreter>-<abi>-<platform>

The "none" ABI will be added if it was not explicitly provided.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._py_interpreter_range
documentation:
Yields Python versions in descending order.

After the latest version, the major-only version will be yielded, and then
all previous versions of that major version.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.compatible_tags
documentation:
Yields the sequence of tags that are compatible with a specific version of Python.

The tags consist of:
- py*-none-<platform>
- <interpreter>-none-any  # ... if `interpreter` is provided.
- py*-none-any

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.mac_platforms
documentation:
Yields the platform tags for a macOS system.

The `version` parameter is a two-item tuple specifying the macOS version to
generate platform tags for. The `arch` parameter is the CPU architecture to
generate platform tags for. Both parameters default to the appropriate value
for the current system.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._is_manylinux_compatible
documentation: From PEP 513, PEP 600

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._glibc_version_string_confstr
documentation: Primary implementation of glibc_version_string using os.confstr.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._glibc_version_string_ctypes
documentation: Fallback implementation of glibc_version_string using ctypes.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._platform_tags
documentation: Provides the platform tags for this installation.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.interpreter_name
documentation: Returns the name of the running interpreter.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.interpreter_version
documentation: Returns the version of the running interpreter.

functions.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.sys_tags
documentation:
Returns the sequence of tag triples for the running interpreter.

The order of the sequence corresponds to priority order for the
interpreter, from most to least important.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags.Tag
documentation:
A representation of the tag triple for a wheel.

Instances are considered immutable and thus are hashable. Equality checking
is also supported.

classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._ELFFileHeader

inner_classes.name: wheel.vendored.packaging.tags._ELFFileHeader._InvalidELFFileHeader
documentation: An invalid ELF file header was found.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executable_and_Linkable_Format#File_header
Python does not provide platform information at sufficient granularity to
identify the architecture of the running executable in some cases, so we
determine it dynamically by reading the information from the running
process. This only applies on Linux, which uses the ELF format.

functions.name: wheel.cli.convert.parse_wininst_info
documentation:
Extract metadata from filenames.

Extracts the 4 metadataitems needed (name, version, pyversion, arch) from
the installer filename and the name of the egg-info directory embedded in
the zipfile (if any).

The egginfo filename has the format:

```
name-ver(-pyver)(-arch).egg-info
```

The installer filename has the format:

```
name-ver.arch(-pyver).exe
```

Some things to note:

1. The installer filename is not definitive. An installer can be renamed
   and work perfectly well as an installer. So more reliable data should
   be used whenever possible.
2. The egg-info data should be preferred for the name and version, because
   these come straight from the distutils metadata, and are mandatory.
3. The pyver from the egg-info data should be ignored, as it is
   constructed from the version of Python used to build the installer,
   which is irrelevant - the installer filename is correct here (even to
   the point that when it's not there, any version is implied).
4. The architecture must be taken from the installer filename, as it is
   not included in the egg-info data.
5. Architecture-neutral installers still have an architecture because the
   installer format itself (being executable) is architecture-specific. We
   should therefore ignore the architecture if the content is pure-python.
